segment-text: & Wash Westmoreland
Current Revisions by Rebecca Lenkiewicz (c) 2017 Colette Film Holdings Ltd EXT. BURGUNDIAN COUNTRYSIDE - SUNRISE - 1892
The first light is breaking over the fields and hedges of this timeless rural landscape. With the clanking of bells, a herd of cows makes its way to the first milking.
INT. COLETTE FAMILY HOME - BEDROOM - EARLY MORNING

The room is simple with a few sticks of worn wooden furniture. A young woman is asleep under the covers, with a cat curled up on the bottom of the bed. This is GABRIELLESIDONIE COLETTE, or as she will later be known, simply COLETTE. A voice calls from down the corridor - her mother SIDO.
Gabrielle!
The girl stirs but her eyes remain stubbornly closed.
(murmuring) Let me sleep!
Come on, Gabrielle!!
Sido is now at the door frame. A handsome country woman, forthright and practical - nobody's fool.
What time is it?
It's 8 o'clock. Willy is coming today.
Colette just murmurs.
She disappears. After a moment, a head emerges from under the pillow and looks to the empty doorway. We get our first look at the milky skinned, precocious, whip-smart, Colette.
EXT. COUNTRY TRAIN STATION - DAY
A train belches steam as it slowly moves toward the station. A MAN IN A TOP HAT leans out of the window. COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
EXT. TRAIN CARRIAGE - DAY
Dismounting, Willy sees a waiting carriage across the platform.
INT. COLETTE FAMILY HOUSE - PARLOR

Wearing her best frock, Colette carries a large TEA SERVICE down a corridor and into the main room. Willy is seated, in conversation with Sido and JULES, the patriarch, an affable old soldier, with one leg, who is smoking a pipe.
And how is Paris these days, Willy?

It's a hot bed! It's "Electric!" Heaving with artists, writers, poets, all seeking to say something profound... most of them are too young or crazed but still, they do generate a certain... life force!
Colette, her hair neatly plaited, puts the cups out for everyone then takes over the teapot and pours.
We were going to see a play in Paris - weren't we? La Tosca.

I was at the opening. I wouldn't bother, quite frankly. Sarah Bernhardt did her best, she always does, but the piece is too overwrought in a particularly feminine way... melodramatic in extremis! Tears on the
stage but all dry eyes in the house.
Maybe I'll go and make up my own mind.
Well, just remember... if a book bores you, you can throw it away. If a painting is garish, you close your eyes. (MORE)
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Whereas bad theatre is like dentistry; you are compelled to stay in the chair, having your skull drilled, until the entire grizzly procedure is over.
Good point, Willy. Painful business!
Though I imagine it must hold a secret source of enjoyment - for the dentist.
Willy raises an appreciative eyebrow and chuckles.
Indeed... Oh - I almost forgot. A small gift.
He reaches in his pocket and pulls out a small package. She unwraps it to find a SNOW GLOBE OF PARIS.
Thank you. I've read about these. Shake it.
She does and looks, fascinated, at the twirling flakes.
Look, Sido. A snow globe.
She holds it up for her mother.
EXT. COLETTE FAMILY HOUSE - LATE AFTERNOON
M. Willy bids good-bye to Jules, Sido and Colette, before the carriage moves off. Colette is holding her comfortable old cat which she hands off to her mother.
Sido, I'm going for a walk.
Change your dress first.
I'll stay on the path.
Change it!
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT INT. COLETTE FAMILY HOUSE - STAIRS - MINUTES LATER
Now in a different dress, Colette starts to come down stairs. Hearing her parents talking in the kitchen, she slows up, listening carefully.
There's something about him I find suspect... something disingenuous.
Oh, not in my reckoning... He's a good man. He has a reputation, yes, but there comes a time in a man's life to settle down...and she's the right age for it. She'll be twenty next time around.
I just worry that he won't understand her. That girl said her first word at nine months. She was always reading, way ahead of her class, she was the lead in the school play - every year.
My dear, would anyone be good enough for you? Hmmph.
Now on the ground floor Colette waits outside the door as her parents talk. Sido moves into view by the kitchen sink.
Have you finished the letter to his father?
I keep starting but I can't seem to finish it. A girl without a dowry, -that just doesn't happen in their circles. They may cut him off if he marries her.
So they call it off. The world won't end.
As her father talks, Colette sneaks back up the last five stairs...
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Sido, he clearly adores her - he's visited four times. He'll give her status, not to mention a tremendous cultural life - the theater, the opera. He'll give her Paris for heaven's sakes!
Colette comes clumping down the stairs, slightly too loudly and into the room.
Can I go now?
Yes. Take this - for blackberries. (hands her a pot) I'm making a tart tomorrow. And don't be late back.
(eye roll) I won't...
Sido watches her daughter, not without suspicion, as she takes off across the garden.
EXT. SAINT-SAUVEUR - COUNTRYSIDE - LATE AFTERNOON

Colette runs down a path, then backs up, picks a few prime blackberries, and continues on. She moves through the woods, with the stealth of an animal then breaks out across a broad green meadow towards a barn.
EXT. OLD BARN - LATE AFTERNOON
She approaches the gaping black door of a dilapidated barn.
INT. OLD BARN
Entering the darkness, Colette blinks to re-adjust her eyes. A flutter of wings attracts her attention. She turns to see an ascending pigeon. Then, below, a tired old horse.
Where are you?
Over here. Enjoying the simple pleasures of country life!
Behind a partition, she sees Willy, reclining on a pile of straw. COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
You look at home in the hay.
I think I might be! And you look like a beautiful young country girl. Right there. With the light...What's that?
Colette shows him, putting the bowl between them as she nestles beside him. Her hands are blue with the juice.

Your fingers are stained. (he takes her hand) Mm... Maybe I could just leave Paris behind. (he takes a blackberry) You could feed me berries. I'd catch a fish with my teeth. Then come back here to my cave. Like a bear. Bliss.
I'd like that.
(he caresses her braids) Your hair is a phenomenon.
In the village, they call me "the girl with the hair."
I can see why. I'm sure you're famous here in your village. A little.
She looks down.
In about 40 minutes. But I have to walk to the station.
Normally takes me about 15 minutes. Will probably take you
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Yes. Right. So if my
We have time - if we're quick.
Yes, we do.
He smiles, moved by her beauty, kisses her, then breaks off.
I just can't say how unimaginably happy I am, Gabrielle. So am I.
He holds her by her shoulders firmly.
I adore you.
He kisses her shoulder. Feeling his beard brush against her skin, Colette smiles as they move into a passionate embrace. A nearby horse chews hay as they proceed to take their clothes off.
INT. COLETTE'S BEDROOM. NIGHT.
Colette sprawls out on the bed, her hair in plaits. She is writing a letter in a school exercise book in pencil, resting on an encyclopaedia.

Dear Willy, As I walked home by the river, the light shining through the trees, my heart filled with a kind of delicious pride. After all, you know all those fine women of Paris...so I must have something. (smiles to herself)) I want to wake up next to you. Trace your profile with my finger while you sleep. Know that the day is ours. That life is ours for the taking. We will be so entire and so happy, my love.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT GARDEN - AFTERNOON
At the Wedding Party, Jules is making a toast Colette and Willy in front of a MIXED CROWD of WEDDING GUESTS.

It's the proudest time in a father's life, but also - I now find - one of the saddest. I have to hand over my beloved daughter, who has lit up our lives for so long, with her wit, her brilliance, her beauty. I will miss you, Gabrielle. Almost as much as your mother will...
A murmur of acknowledgement from the assembled. Colette smiles at her father, and catches her mother's eye, holding back emotion.

But it is no small compensation that I give her to a man of such inestimable standing. There are many celebrities in Paris but few are famous enough to be known by -- by a single name. WILLY - Welcome to the Colette family.
Everyone cheers. Colette turns to her new husband,
SOME TIME LATER - EVENING
A COUNTRY BAND strikes up. garden. COUPLES polka around the A COUPLE OF PEASANTS carry trays of drinks from a kitchen tent to the GUESTS. Some CHILDREN chase a chicken through the dancers.
This is definitely a country wedding.
It'll make a good story -- in Paris.
You'll be the story in Paris, Gabrielle. Just wait 'til they get a look at you.
Willy twirls Colette round and round and round...
EXT. CITY OF PARIS - EVENING - 1893 Across the river Seine - the center of the demi-monde; the
Left Bank. This is not postcard Paris - the buildings are sooty and chimneys are filling the sky with smoke. On the quay, PEOPLE go about their business as a YOUNG ATTENDANT lights the gas lamp.
INT. COLETTE AND WILLY'S APARTMENT - BOUDOIR - EVENING
MATILDE, the maid, is winching Colette into a very tight red froufrou dress. A knock on the door.
Are you ready yet?
No - not yet. (to the maid) What do you think, Matilde?
It's pretty, Madame.
Colette looks at herself critically.
INT. BEDROOM/HALLWAY
Willy returns and prepares to knock again.
We should try to get there before
it ends.
I'm coming...
The door opens. Colette is in another dress - the one from their meeting in Saint-Sauveur. Matilde hovers nervously.
Is the carriage waiting?

Willy looks disappointed but together they proceed through SEVERAL ROOMS to the front door. We get a feeling for Willy's apartment - a warren of dark rooms overcrowded by boxes, papers and Wagnerian bric-a-brac. Willy, it seems, is a hoarder.
Where's your new dress?
It looks ridiculous. I couldn't breathe in it.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
That thing cost me two hundred and thirty-seven francs.
I know. We'll get it adjusted.
(half joking) More money!
They exit.
EXT. ELEGANT PARISIAN STREET - EARLY EVENING
The carriage pulls up. Colette is fiddling with a spot of tooth powder on her dress.
What is it?
...It's tooth paste.
(amused) My God! Here...
He rubs a little spit on it.
That'll do. Let's go in...
INT. MME. DE CAILLAVET'S SALON - HALLWAY
They approach the giant door.
Ready for the lions...?
She lets out a nervous exhale. The double doors glide open.
INT. MADAME DE CAILLAVET'S SALON - MAIN ROOM

Willy and Colette proceed into a reception room filled with the beautiful, the sparkling, and the eccentric of Paris' Golden Age. The mirrors have been polished; so have the epigrams. Amidst the gas lamps, the India rubber plants, and the zebra skin rugs, the energy is bouncing off the walls. Willy acknowledges hellos and nods to people along the way. With her tight braids and country dress, Colette looks drab in this sea of peacocks.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
They arrive at a circle of conversation -- that of MME. DE CAILLAVET, the hostess, the debonair COUNT MUFFAT, and a few SALONITES and FRIENDS.
It's monstrous. I can't look at it.
At least it's temporary.
Oh here's Willy -- Willy! Eiffel's Tower, are you for or against?
I'm for it, if a little jealous of this giant erection in the heart of our capital... belonging to someone else.
Laughter from the circle.
You rogue, Willy. And you've brought a... an orphaned relative?
A secret love child?
(acknowledging their mockery) Very good... May I present to you my wife, Gabrielle-Sidonie
Colette curtsies politely, her cheeks flushing.
Pleased to meet you.
Delighted. Well, astonished actually. You have caught the slipperiest eel... How does one do that?
I'm not sure...
Colette smiles, a little nervously.
Where are you from, you sweet thing?
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
I'm from Saint-Sauveur-en-Puisaye... (no reaction) ...it's in Burgundy.
Hence the dress!
Have you "relocated," Willy? This is apparently hilarious and there is laughter.
Tst...ignore him... But how on earth did you two meet?
Our fathers served together in the army...
(riding over her) Willy married! The wild days are done, eh?
You're quite mistaken, sir. The wild days have just begun.
Willy smiles at his new bride's moxie. A business-like young man rushes up to Willy. This is PIERRE VEBER.
Willy, thank God you're back. That short story for La Forgue -
Pierre. Let me introduce you to my wife, Gabrielle.
What..? Very pleased to meet you.
Before she can speak, he turns to Willy with a more urgent tone.
It's due on Tuesday and you haven't given me the slightest clue.
Amuse yourself for a moment, Gabrielle.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Willy draws Veber aside to continue the shop talk. WILLY

sketch. Small coastal resort, damp bathing-costumes. The perfect place to rejuvenate a de-iodized constitution. A buxom girl approaches, long legs... Colette is left standing by herself. Nearby, a silver tray displays a living turtle with an exquisitely jeweled shell. She takes a lettuce leaf from a salad dish and feeds it.
Poor thing. You want the earth and the grass don't you?
Across the room she sees Willy, with Veber, lifting the glass on a wall candle and lighting a cigar. Feeling out of sorts, she moves through to an adjacent room where...
INT. MME. DE CAILLAVET'S SALON - MUSIC ROOM

A STRING QUARTET is giving a recital to the assembled. Across the room a group of SALONITES are clearly discussing her. Embarrassed, Colette finds an empty seat. She looks across the room - the salonites are still whispering.
Are your ears burning?
Colette looks up and sees - Marcel SCHWOB, an intellectual with a humane gleam in his eye. He sits next to her.
I'm Schwob, a friend of Willy's.
Hello. I'm Gabrielle. Willy described you to me.
I hope he was kind.
(wryly) Hope costs nothing.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Schwob raises an amused eyebrow, then looks out into the room where people are stealing glances at them.
The modern day mob, aren't they? (Colette nods) But their goddess is gossip instead of the guillotine. Are you feeling overwhelmed? A little.
They may look grand and intimidating. But just imagine them shitting. Each and every one of them.
Colette looks at him, incredulous.
Well they all do, don't they?
Across the room, AN IMPORTANT-LOOKING GENTLEMAN, waves to
(to the Gentleman) Good evening, Sir! (to Colette) Try it.
They may look grand and intimidating. But just imagine them perched over a chamber pot. Each and every one of them.
INT. MADAME DE CAILLAVET'S SALON - VARIOUS ROOMS

We follow Colette as she searches for Willy in the sea of SOCIALITES. She walks through rooms where PEOPLE are now dancing in an avant garde fashion then through a series of SMALLER COMPARTMENTS, where SERVANTS are polishing glasses and CHEFS are preparing hors d'oeuvres, past palms and oil lamps, to a distant room at the far end of the salon.
INT. MME. DE CAILLAVET'S SALON - SMOKING ROOM

From the doorway, Colette spies Willy leaning on the mantelpiece talking to a beautiful YOUNG WOMAN in a red dress. The woman leans in close to him, laughing. He whispers something and she laughs even harder.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
There's a shrieking coming from the corner of the main room. Colette turns to sees the crowd laughing as a BOHEMIAN pours milk into an upright piano.
INT. HORSE-DRAWN CARRIAGE - MOVING - NIGHT
Colette looks out the window into the night-time streets. After a moment, Willy ventures a question.
So what did you think of the salon?
That was essentially - what you witnessed tonight - everyone who's anyone in Paris... Was it..?
Who did you like? Who did you talk to?
I liked the tortoise. I think he was as bored as I was.
I suspect you were more intimidated than bored?
No. I found them...shallow. Pretentious.

Ah, you're reading them wrongly. It's not pretension so much as exaggeration. The ideal is to be authentic yet larger than life. To present a personality with a capital P. You could do it too with your country girl charm ...
She turns to him.
(interrupting) Did that woman have a capital P?
Which one?
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
The one you were talking to? In the red dress. With the breasts?
Nicole D'Allier? That was just... flirtation. It means absolutely nothing.
Then why do it?
It's what one does... to pass the time. It staves off boredom. You're reading it all completely wrong.
Colette sighs, frustrated. Willy changes tack.
Forget about all of them now. Any way haven't you heard the news? Willy is married. Happily married. And he's going home with his bride, who he loves very much.
Colette nods, looks at him with a half-smile. He is forgiven.
EXT. PARIS STREET
The carriage rides off into the distance.
INT. COLETTE AND WILLY'S BUILDING - STAIRCASE - MORNING
From directly above, we see Pierre Veber dash up the stairs.
INT. COLETTE AND WILLY'S APARTMENT - ENTRANCE ROOM
Colette is seated at a desk, carefully roughing out a She writes a line then crosses it out, rephrases
There's a knock at the door. Veber enters in the hallway and looks through to Colette's small room where she sits with a large pile of correspondence.
Hello, Veber.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Gabrielle. It's cold out there today.
I'm under house arrest so it doesn't affect me. (gestures to the other room)
Schwob's already in there. We follow Veber through the maze-like apartment to Willy's office. He passes through the front room which is semi-dark, its furniture covered by dust sheets.
INT. WILLY'S STUDY
Willy, mid-flow, barely notices Veber enter. Schwob sits by an oil lamp, taking notes.
So something like..."all the humor and
vivacity descends into...a dreadful
the audience pale, and nauseous as though having eaten a bad oyster."
It's a bit extreme. The ending was actually quite touching.
Willy thinks for a moment.
Hmmph - I prefer my version.
He finally acknowledges the new arrival.
Willy. Schwob.
(to Schwob) So drop in those embellishments and file it at The Echo before
Will do. (he leaves.)
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
The factory's on fire... We're on fire...! Sit. I've had a remarkable idea - it could be, anyway...
What is it?
I'm going to launch a novel. What?!
We've had stories by Willy, music reviews by Willy. Now Willy's first novel. There'll be enough literature in it to appeal to the high-brows and enough filth for the great unwashed.
And who's going to write it?
...You are!
Veber shakes his head, absorbing this.
Brandy? Cigar?
INT. ENTRANCE ROOM
Schwob stops to chat to Colette by the desk. Matilde is stoking the fire.
Are you writing for him too? He's made you one of his ghosts already?
Just letters...
You're fastidious... exacting.
A lot of crossing out, you mean.
...well, yes.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Willy copies them out afterwards so they are in his handwriting.
No one has handwriting like M. Willy.
INT. WILLY'S STUDY
Willy is completely absorbed in his pitch, sweat beading from his forehead as he paces.
He first sees her, Monna, held aloft on the shoulders of her admirers.. She's

a genius - he's captivated by her. She seduces him in her shabby rooms - five entire days of carnal bliss - but she has a powerful hold over him. She instinctively understands his base desires. We wonder - will Renaud escape go back to his wholesome friends and his burgeoning career - or will he be dragged down by her toxic embrace, forgetting all
Willy ends with a flourish, eyes slightly damp.
INT. COLETTE AND WILLY'S APARTMENT
Colette gets up from her desk, as Willy escorts Veber through the apartment.
How long do you think it will take?
Two or three months?
No no...weeks, Veber! It has to be weeks! Write for four hours at a time...
All right... I'll come back to you if I get stuck.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Yes...Just write it...and fast! Let's get some money coming in.
Goodbye, Gabrielle.
She waves. The door closes on Veber. A piece of wallpaper has pulled free of the wall. Willy rubs it back in place.
Chaos. Working in these conditions. Absolute...chaos.
Willy comes over to his wife and kisses her as they walk back into the front room.
You've married a literary entrepreneur. What a phenomenal disaster.
You've married a country girl. Not a penny to her name.
We're utterly doomed aren't we?
They both laugh. She hands him the rough draft of the letter. He peruses it quickly.
Perfect... but how about; "The contract you have sent me seems eminently fair, with only one shortcoming - the money." Always pump something up before you prick it. You get more of a "POP" that way.
Unexpectedly, Willy farts. Willy!
Intimacy in all its savage abandon, my love! I've a few meetings today. Let's meet at La Mascotte at nine. No make it ten.
...Goodbye Willy.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Colette watches him go. MUSIC COMES UP FOR THE NEXT SEQUENCE It is greenish, uneven, and mottled. Using a tiny pair of scissors, a hand is etching the letter "W."
INT. LA MASCOTTE - NIGHT
Willy, Colette, Schwob, Veber and a COQUETTE, are finishing up a meal, drinking champagne. Willy is regaling them with an anecdote as he pays the bill with a roll of notes.
INT. THEATRE - NIGHT
As Willy walks down the aisle toward his seat, he is in his element. He nods acknowledgements and greetings to everybody who is anybody. Colette follows a couple of steps behind.
INT. COLETTE AND WILLY'S APARTMENT - ENTRANCE ROOM - DAY 33
We see the etching progress - W I L. The creator is none other than Colette, alone, absorbed in this labor of love.
INT. LIVING ROOM - DAY
Colette starts pulling sheets off old furniture in the living room and opening up the blinds.
INT. BEDROOM - NIGHT
In bed together, Willy is reading the evening paper as Colette nuzzles into his chest. He indicates a cartoon drawing - of himself with his trademark top hat - that Colette finds amusing.
INT. ENTRANCE ROOM - DAY
Colette completes the etching of W I L L Y on the kitchen window, taking a moment to admire her handiwork. MUSIC ENDS.
EXT. RUE JACOB - AFTERNOON
Colette walks towards home, turns into the courtyard and collects the mail.
INT. COLETTE AND WILLY'S APARTMENT - HALLWAY/LIVING ROOM 38
She enters, already opening the envelope and walks through the house perusing the letter - that is written in green ink. COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
(murmuring) No...you're a liar.
Disgusted, she crumples it and throws it to the ground.
EXT. STREETS OF PARIS - AFTERNOON

We follow close on Colette as she walks determinedly. The streets seem abrasive, dirty, polluted. Several STREET WALKERS ignore her as she walks by. She checks the address on the crumpled letter, looks up at the building.
EXT. COURTYARD - LATE AFTERNOON

Colette walks across the courtyard, up the stairs, across a balcony and tries the door to a small apartment. It's locked. Then she hears a familiar voice and moves over to look through a small window.
INT. LOTTE KINCELER'S APARTMENT - LATE AFTERNOON
That's 153 plus 278 plus 34 plus 106 for your hats... That makes it 571. For one month, Lotte...
Do you want me to look cheap?
Willy is with the voluptuous LOTTE KINCELER. the damning evidence: an accounting ledger. Between them
But for that money I could be laid five times as often by a prostitute.
EXT. LOTTE'S KINCELER'S APARTMENT - LATE AFTERNOON

There's the sound of a woman's raucous laughter. Colette turns to see TWO SEX-WORKERS across the courtyard. As she turns back, Willy sees her at the window. Lotte instinctively grabs a pair of dressmakers scissors.
Ah, my dear. You've come to fetch me.
EXT. COURTYARD - LATE AFTERNOON
Now walking back down the stairs, Colette is struggling mightily to keep her emotions in check as Willy blusters.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
She's not a disreputable woman. There's even a play about her - it's a shit play but nevertheless... I wonder who the hell wrote that letter?
I don't care who wrote it.

She's no rival to you, Gabrielle. I promise, I'll never sleep with her again. But - you have to understand - this is what men do. We are the weaker sex. We don't have your strength. We are simply slaves to our urges. And here in the city - it's perfectly acceptable to...
Colette flies at him with inarticulate rage.
I don't accept it! You've been lying to me. All this time! I wait for you all day long! And I never ask you for anything because you say we have no money.
It's true - we have no money.
Because you spend it all on her!
I really don't!
Then when you get into our bed...and I touch you...and I kiss you...and you say you're too tired...every night...no! No!
I've been inattentive. I'll make amends.
He reaches out to her.
Don't you dare touch me!
She spits out the words with such venom that they bring him to a halt as she walks on.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Gabrielle! I gave up my inheritance for you! My bloody freedom!
Go to hell!
She exits through the large beaten-up gateway.
EXT. RURAL TRAIN STATION - DAY
The train pulls into the station. A solitary figure waits on the platform - Sido.
EXT. COLETTE FAMILY HOUSE - BACKYARD - DAY
Sido and Colette are hanging out laundry. Colette seems to be only half-listening - her mind is elsewhere.
...the Laurent girl had her baby
Really? Why?
He inherited a house. In Lorraine. I'll miss them.
Colette manages a half smile.
I'm glad that you're home. How long do you think you'll stay?
I don't know, Sido.
It must seem boring to you. Compared to Paris.
No. Not at all.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT INT. COLETTE FAMILY HOUSE - PARLOR - DAY
Colette is stretched out on a sofa, reading a book, absently stroking a cat in her lap. A loud clock is ticking. Sido appears at the doorway.
You must eat something.
I'm not hungry.
You're always hungry. Gabrielle... you're not..?
No. I'll eat later.
Sido turns to leave but looks back at Colette.
Come outside. Help me with the tomatoes.
EXT. SIDO'S KITCHEN GARDEN - DAY
Colette and Sido are kneeling together in the soil. They tie some young tomato plants. An ANT crawls up one of the stems.
Agh - ants. There must be honeydew...
(feels the leaf) Yes.. it's sticky.
The white flies have probably got to it.
They'll have sucked all the juice.
We need some ladybirds?
Or a spider. There's a big one that's dangling in my room. I'll catch her and put her to work out here.
Colette stops, looks at Sido.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Did you ever feel like you were playing a part, Sido?
In what way?
As a wife... or a mother... That you were just going through the motions?
Sido thinks. Inspects the other leaves.
Sometimes as a wife.. Never as a mother. Why?
Colette shakes her head, perturbed.
Is he good to you Gabri..? (Colette's eyes tear up) What is it, my love?
Colette finally opens up...
Nothing's how I imagined it.
Come here...
Colette falls into her mother.
My darling kitten. No one can take away what you are - no one. You're too strong for that. Always have been. Trust no one but yourself. I know.
What's he done to upset you?
Nothing... Nothing. (she pulls away) It's all - just - new... That's all. must get used to marriage.
Sido nods, looks at her daughter who looks away. COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT
Better to make marriage get used to you.
EXT. SAINT SAUVEUR - RIVER BANK - DAY
Willy and Colette walk along the dappled banks of the green slow river. She is distant. He is persistent.
Are you happy to be here?
Yes. It makes the city seem even more foul...
Well, you are a country girl...
She rolls her eyes.
Did you miss me?
(lies) No, not really.
Life has been...awful without you. Just dead.
His tone grows more emotional. She holds firm.

In 1911, Colette's "The Vagabond," based on her music hall experience, was published under her own name to great critical acclaim.
Missy and Colette continued their relationship for many years. Missy often accompanied Colette on her music hall tours, though never again opted to appear on the stage.
Willy continued to publish ghostwritten books with Meg, but failed to replicate the success of the Claudines. He died in 1931 in relative obscurity.

Paul Heon did not destroy the original Claudine manuscripts but returned them to Colette. They became crucial evidence in a 1948 legal battle to establish the true authorship of the novels... that Colette won.
COLETTE - FINAL SHOOTING SCRIPT

Colette went on to publish over fifty acclaimed novels and short stories. She became the most celebrated female author in the history of French literature. In her old age, Colette remarked, "What a wonderful life I've had. I only wish I'd realized it sooner."
